title: The Man Whom She Preferred
subtitle: Women's Choice, Divorce, and Family Reckoning in Tebtunis, 107-123 CE
summary: In Roman Tebtunis, a young woman named Kronous also called Philoumene dissolved her marriage and recovered her dowry, gaining the legal right to marry 'the man whom she prefers.' Around her, other women in the same village fought over dowries, unwritten marriages, disputed infants, jewelry, houses, and the right to begin again. Their love stories survive not in poems but in divorce deeds, settlements, and family accounts.

sections.title: The Price of a Daughter
sections.body_html:
Nine years after Kronous’s divorce, another Tebtunis document recorded the delayed settling of a woman’s marriage portion. In 122 CE, Didymarion, daughter of Herakleides son of Maron, acknowledged that her two brothers, Herakleides also called Valerius and Lysimachos, had paid her 600 silver drachmae still owed from what their dead father had promised her, in addition to 500 silver drachmae already granted in the marriage contract.4

The document is one of the best surviving measurements of what a daughter cost a family in Roman Egypt. Didymarion’s marriage had required cash, and not a little of it. The two brothers pay in the presence of their mother Didyme, then about seventy-five years old, who adds her own contribution: a pair of gold earrings with genuine pearls, a purple garment, a tunic, and a guarantee that part of a house and courtyard in Tebtunis had already been conveyed to her daughter.4

This is not romantic language. It is inventory. But inventory is often where love survives. The earrings, the purple garment, the dwelling rights, the years-late payment of money once promised by a dead father: these are material traces of how a woman was sent into marriage and how her natal family remained bound to her afterward.4

The names in the deed matter too. One of the brothers paying Didymarion is Herakleides also called Valerius, the same unusual double name carried by the child mentioned in Kronous’s divorce deed nine years earlier.14 The identification cannot be proved beyond doubt, but it is highly plausible: the same village, the right chronology, the same name pair, and a family environment in which names circulated across generations.4 If the identification is right, the infant of Kronous’s failed marriage grew up into a man who spent his middle age helping settle the marriage obligations of another woman in the family.

That is what the Tebtunis archive does to time. It refuses to let marriage be a single event. It keeps showing its afterlife. A father promises money in one deed. He dies. The daughter remains owed. The sons pay years later. The mother adds jewelry and clothing. The marriage is still being assembled after the father is gone.4

sections.title: The Dead Daughter and the Dead Child
sections.body_html:
The harshest of the Tebtunis papers from these years concerns a woman who did not live long enough to collect anything back.

In 120 or 121 CE, a settlement was drawn up over Apia also called Herakleia, daughter of Herakleia also called Isidora and her late husband Kronion.5 Apia had been living with Herakleides son of Sarapion in what the papyrus calls a marriage without written contract.5 During that union she became pregnant and gave birth. Then she died.

After her death, her mother’s family and the father of the child went to court before the strategos. The mother’s side claimed that the child born to Apia had died and that Herakleides had taken another infant in its place. He answered that the child he carried away was indeed Apia’s own child. The dispute rose high enough that the papyrus refers to a formal record made in the strategos’s presence and to the possibility of a higher court.5

Then comes one of the bleakest sentences in the archive. By the time the matter was settled, the text says, the infant itself, either of Apia also called Herakleia or a strange one, had also died.5

The entire controversy collapses there. A dead young mother. A disputed infant. A family trying to prove that the father’s child was not the real child. A father insisting that it was. And then the child too is gone, whether real or substituted. The parties settle because no living body remains to fight over.5

If one wanted a romance from the papyri in the modern sense, one would turn away from this document. It is too brutal, too administrative, too stripped of inner life. But if one wants a microhistory of what love, sex, and attachment looked like when they passed through the legal machinery of Roman Egypt, this is exactly the evidence to stay with. The unwritten marriage mattered enough to generate litigation. The child mattered enough to generate a contest over identity. The dead woman’s maternal family mattered enough to challenge the father’s claim. Marriage here was not only emotional or sexual. It was also a site of jurisdiction.5

And if Apia’s union had been unwritten, that too tells us something. Not every household relation in Roman Egypt rested on a fully registered contract. People lived together, had children, and only later, when death or dispute came, discovered what the lack of paperwork would cost them.56

sections.title: Sixteen and Free Again
sections.body_html:
In 123 CE, another girl from Tebtunis was released.

Thenstotoetis, about sixteen years old, together with her father and former husband, formally annulled the marriage that had been established between her and Lysas, a freedman of Herakleia daughter of Lysimachos.7 The contract had been made only two years earlier, in the fifth year of Hadrian. Now it was cancelled. Her father recovered 300 silver drachmae together with all the parapherna. The document insists that each party may now dispose of his or her own property, and that Thenstotoetis may join herself in marriage again, to whatever man she wishes, without accusation from Lysas.7

The echo of Kronous’s divorce clause is unmistakable.17 Different parties, different amount, different age, but the same legal imagination: a marriage can be dissolved; the dowry can be recovered; the young woman can be made available, lawfully and explicitly, for another match of her own future.

This should not be romanticized. Thenstotoetis was sixteen. Her father acted as her guardian. The men around her negotiated the money. The scribal formula of whatever man she wishes did not mean modern individual freedom.7 It meant something narrower and more local: that the old husband had no legal hold on her, and that the natal family could now direct or approve another marriage without interference from the first.

Even so, the clause is not meaningless. The papyrus does not say to whichever man her father chooses. It says to whatever man she wishes.7 The formula does not erase paternal power, but neither does it simply collapse the woman into it. Again and again, the Tebtunis divorces preserve a small language of female choice at the precise point where the first marriage has been made void.17

sections.title: Why the Money Had to Come Back
sections.body_html:
Dowry return was the practical hinge on which these stories turned. A woman might leave a marriage because the union had become impossible, but leaving without recovering the dowry was financially devastating. In Roman Egypt, dowry was not an optional gift. It was a transfer into the marriage that remained, in principle, tied to the woman’s side and recoverable on dissolution.6

That principle appears everywhere in the Egyptian evidence. Syra, petitioning in Oxyrhynchus sometime between 20 and 50 CE, told the chief judge that her husband had squandered her 200 drachmae dowry, abused her, and finally deserted her, and she demanded repayment with the statutory increase.2 Makaria, in a late Ptolemaic petition, complained that her husband had thrown her out of the house and still had not restored the dowry.8 Zois, separating from Antipatros in the Augustan period, acknowledged receipt of her garments and a pair of gold earrings before the old marriage was voided and both parties freed to marry again.9

Kronous’s deed belongs squarely in that world.1 Her father acknowledges receipt of the gold, silver, and paraphernalia. Only after that do the parties swear off future suits and name her freedom to remarry.1 The sequence matters. Property comes first because property makes separation survivable.

The same logic shadows Didymarion’s receipt in 122 CE.4 Her marriage settlement was not over when she married. It remained open until the rest of what was promised had been handed over, item by item, relative by relative. The papyrus is what happens when the economics of affection lag behind the ceremony.

sections.title: A Village of Women Beginning Again
sections.body_html:
If we read the Tebtunis papers one by one, they seem narrow and technical. If we read them together, a pattern emerges.

Kronous leaves a marriage at twenty and recovers enough to begin again.1 Apia dies in an unwritten marriage, and her family fights over the baby she left behind.5 Didymarion receives the last of what her father owed her marriage years after his death.4 Thenstotoetis, not yet fully grown, leaves her first husband and is restored to legal availability for another union.7 Didyme, the seventy-five-year-old mother in the Didymarion deed, appears not as passive background but as a woman still disposing of garments, pearls, and real property in the service of her daughter’s future.4

This is not one love story. It is a social world in which women kept being pushed back to the same threshold: the point where a marriage had gone wrong, or been cut short by death, and the question became how much of a future could be retrieved from the wreckage.

The Roman state cared about this mainly as a matter of registration and enforceable claims. The Tebtunis notaries cared because their office existed to turn fragile understandings into valid documents. But the people in these papers cared for another reason. A marriage was not only a status. It was a transfer of money, clothing, jewelry, labor, and reproductive expectation. To undo it was to try to rescue a woman’s place in the world before she was swallowed by someone else’s household.

That is why the most hopeful line in the archive is also one of the shortest.

The man whom she prefers.1

We do not know whether Kronous ever married him. We do not know whether he already existed in her mind when the divorce was written up, or whether the phrase was no more than a scribal formula applied equally to women who desired and women who merely endured. We do not know what she felt when the document was read out, or whether she was relieved, ashamed, angry, or eager.

But the clause would not have mattered if there had been no world in which preference mattered. The papyrus preserves the legal shell of an emotional fact: that after one marriage was over, there remained the possibility, however constrained, that a woman might choose differently the next time.1

The village record office of Tebtunis was not in the business of recording love. It recorded scars, sums, receipts, cancellations, guardians, and rights of action. Yet by accident, and by accumulation, it recorded something else as well: not romance exactly, but female second chances.

sources.translation:
Copy of divorce. The 17th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva
                         Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus month - - at M - - in the Polemon-district of the
                         Arsinoite nome.  Kronous also called Philoumene, daughter of Kastor, 20 years old, having
                         a scar on the right shin, with the assistance of her own father, Kastor, son of Pyrion, aged
                         44 years, having a scar on the right of the forehead, - - and Lysimachos, former husband
                         of Kronous also called Philoumene, son of Herakleides, aged 30 years, having a scar on
                         the left knee, - - that they have dissolved their marriage with one another, - - which had
                         existed between them on the strength of a deed of acknowledgement written in former
                         times in the record-office at Tebtunis. Each of them shall be free to manage his own affairs
                         as he wishes, and Kronous to conclude a new marriage with the man whom she prefers,
                         (without intervenience?) of Lysimachos.  And Kastor mentioned above acknowledges
                         that he has forthwith received from Lysimachos, the husband of his daughter Kronous
                         - - through the (record-office) at Tebtunis the dowry in gold and silver and - -.  And none
                         of the aforesaid persons shall proceed against one another in any way, neither on account
                         of the possessions belonging to the marriage nor on account of any other matter written
                         or unwritten, a debt or an agreement whatsoever, from the former times up to the present
                         day.  To Lysimachos and Kronous is born from one another a son named Herakleides also
                         called Valerius. Nullified are the bonds which the two, Lysimachos and Kastor, have from
                         one another.  Signatory for Kronous also called Philoumene is her aforesaid father, Kastor,
                         son of Pyrion, who is also appointed as her guardian. This act of divorce shall be valid
                         wheresoever it is produced - - I Kronous also called Philoumene, with the assistance of
                         my father, Kastor, son of Pyrion, and - - Kastor already mentioned, we acknowledge that
                         we have dissolved the marriage - - which had existed with Lysimachos on the strength
                         of a deed of acknowledgement written in former times in the record-office at Tebtunis.
                         I, Kastor, have received the
               
            
            
               
                   agreed dowry in gold and silver mentioned in it, and various
                           paraphernalia. And each of us shall be free etc. (Similar subscription of Lysimachos).
                           Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 13

Copy. To Boukolos, ex-agoranomus, ex-priest of Alexander
                         founder of the city and of the classes, priest, chief-judge, and curator of the chrematistae
                         and the other courts, through his son, Sarapion, also ex-priest of Alexander founder of
                         the city and of the classes, temporarily performing the duties of chief-judge, from Herakleia
                         also called Isidora, daughter of Mysthes, who is absent and on whose behalf her brother,
                         Ptolemaios, son of Mysthes, is acting by virtue of an agreement done through the registrar’s
                         office at Ptolemais Euergetis in the Arsinoite nome, in the month of Epeiph of the last
                         year, and from Ptolemaios himself and Lysimachos, son of Didymos, son of Lysimachos,
                         whose mother is Apia, and from Herakleides, son of Sarapion, son of Herakleides, all of
                         them from the metropolis of the Arsinoite nome.
                 Whereas Herakleides has been married without written contract with Apia also called
                         Herakleia, daughter of Herakleia also called Isidora, and the latter’s deceased husband,
                         Kronion, brother of Lysimachos, and as during the marriage she became pregnant through
                         him and was delivered, the child - - -; (whereas), moreover, Apia also called Herakleia
                         having died, Herakleia also called Isidora and her said brother, Ptolemaios, and Lysimachos
                         and (on the other side) Herakleides have attended before Eudemos, strategos of the Herakleides
                         district in the Arsinoite nome, the party of Herakleia also called Isidora argued that
                         the child borne by Apia also called Herakleia had died and that Herakleides had adopted
                         another one;  Herakleides himself, however, argued that the child which he had taken
                         with him was really the child born by Apia also called Herakleia, as appears according
                         to their own information from the record drawn up in the presence of the strategos in
                         the month of Epeiph of the 4th year of Hadrian the lord, which proves also that the case
                         has been left to the higher court.  Since it has happened, as both parties concede, that the
                         infant itself, either of Apia also called Herakleia or a strange one, has died, they have now
                         settled the whole controversy and agree with each other, viz. Herakleia also called Isidora
                         and Ptolemaios and Lysimachos, that they will not proceed against Herakleides neither
                         on account of the charge taken down in the minutes of the strategos nor of any other
                         matter with or without written
               
            
            
               
                   document from past times until today; and the other
                           party, Herakleides, that he will proceed neither against Herakleia also called Isidora and
                           Ptolemaios and Lysimachos nor against any of the possessions left by Apia also called
                           Herakleia, neither on the strength of the minutes nor of any other - - -;  Herakleia also
                           called Isidora and Ptolemaios and Lysimachos suffering no loss in any other debt contracted
                           with or without a written document. We request.  (Date).
                           Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 20

sources.translation:
The 6th year of Imperator Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus,
                      the month of Kaisareios 1st, at Tebtunis in the Polemon district of the Arsinoite nome.
                Didymarion, daughter of Herakleides, aged about 48 years, having no distinguishing
                      marks, with the assistance of Kronion, son of Lysanias, her nephew on mother’s side, aged
                      about 52 years, having a scar on the left side of the neck, acknowledges to her brothers
                      from the same father and the same mother, Herakleides also called Valerius, aged about
                      44 years, having a scar on the right knee, and Lysimachos, aged about 38 years, having
                      a scar on the left knee, in the presence of our common mother, Didyme, daughter of Lysimachos,
                      aged about 75 years, having a scar on the left brow, with the assistance of one
                      of her sons, Lysimachos,  that Didymarion has received from her two brothers from hand
                      to hand the 600 silver drachmae promised by our common father, Herakleides, son of Maron,
                      who is dead, beside those (valuables) which he (gave) her through the marriage-contract
                      drawn up by the same registrar’s office in the 9th year of the deified Trajan, above the
                      dowry of 500 silver drachmae which he granted her through the same marriage-contract,
                      the full total being 1100 drachmae, and through an agreement of the father in the 10th
                      year of the deified Trajan, 6th supplementary day through the same office;  and neither
                      Didymarion nor her representatives shall proceed against the two brothers nor against
                      one of them nor against their representatives, neither on account of these (payments),
                      nor on account of any other written or unwritten (obligation) or debt or any agreement
                      whatsoever - - - from the past times until the present day, without any excuse.  And
                      moreover, Didyme, their mother, agrees that she has forthwith endowed her daughter,
                      Didymarion, with a pair of golden earrings with genuine pearls of four quarters (in weight)
                      and a purple garment to be worn with (?) one tunic, all which gifts Didymarion has received;
              and on compulsion Didyme herself shall guarantee immediately for her daughter
                      Didymarion as neither alienated nor encumbered in any way the half of the house and
                      courtyard in Tebtunis which she already gave her through the said contract dated in the
                      10th year of Trajan, 6th supplementary day.  (Subscriptions of i. Didymarion, written by
                      Kronion,  ii. of Lysimachos and Didyme, written by Lysimachos,  iii. of Herakleides-Valerius.
              Docket of registrar).
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 21

(From J.E. Grubbs, Women and the Law in the Roman Empire, 2002, p. 213.)  
In the seventh year of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, 21 Phamenoth, in Tebtunis of the Polemon division of the Arsinoite nome.  Thenstotoetis, about sixteen years old, without distinguishing marks, with her kyrios her father Heron son of Neilos about forty-four years old, with a scar on the little finger of his left hand, and the same Heron, and the one who was the husband of Thenstotoetis,  Lysas the freedman of Herakleia the daughter of Lysimachos, about twenty-two years old, with a scar on his right cheek, and Sosas, son of Areotos, about fifty-four years old, with a scar on his right shin – these four acknowledge to each other that Thenstotoetis and Lysas have jointly  annulled their marriage with each other, which was established by them (and) completed by virtue of a written agreement (homologia) of marriage through the same record-office in the fifth year of our lord Hadrian Caesar, … day missing Hathur, and they have also presented this [agreement] for the purpose of having it   annulled and invalidated. 
	And (they acknowledge) that Heron has back from Sosas the three hundred drachmas of silver (given) through the dowry agreement and all the parapherna. And that it is possible for each [of them] to dispose [his own] things as  he or she chooses, and (it is possible) for Thenstotoetis to join herself in marriage again, to [whatever] man she wishes, not [being] open to accusation from Lysas. And that they will not bring charges against each other [about] any aspect of the things connected with the marriage nor about any matter in general  up to the present day. [Subscriber] Marepsemis son of Pakebkis, [about] forty-eight years old with a scar on his nose. 
	[written in a second hand] Lysas, freedman of Herakleia, [and] Sosas son of Areotos … that they have jointly annulled the marriage with Thenstouetis sic, and that they  will not bring charges against each other in general. Marepsemis son of Pakebkis wrote on their behalf since they do not know letters. 
	in a third hand Thenstotoetis with her kyrios her father Heron – I have annulled the marriage and I have back the dowry. Heron –  I wrote on her behalf because she does not know letters.
	in a fourth hand I have signed it.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Heraclides, priest, chief justice, superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts, from Syra, daughter of Theon. I married Sarapion, bringing him by cession a dowry amounting to 200 drachmae of silver. As he was destitute of means I received him into my parents’ house, and I for my part conducted myself blamelessly in all respects. But Sarapion, having squandered my dowry as he pleased, continually ill-treated and insulted me, using violence towards me, and depriving me of the necessaries of life; finally he deserted me leaving me in a state of destitution. I therefore beg you to order him to be brought before you, in order that he may be compelled perforce to pay back my dowry increased by half its amount. This petition is without prejudice to any other claims which I have or may have against him.

sources.translation:
Kollema (pasted sheet).

To Protarchos, from Zois daughter of Herakleides, with her guardian, her brother Eirenaios son of Herakleides, and from Antipatros son of Zenon.

Zois and Antipatros agree that they have separated from one another from the marriage that had been established between them by agreement through the same tribunal in the present 17th year of Caesar, in Hathyr. Zois acknowledges that she has received back from Antipatros, by hand from his house, the garments she had as her dowry portion, valued at 120 silver drachmai, and a pair of gold earrings. The marriage agreement is hereby void from this point. Zois shall not proceed, nor shall anyone else on her behalf, against Antipatros for recovery of the dowry, and neither party shall proceed against the other regarding the marriage or any other matter whatsoever up to the present day. From this day, Zois shall be free to join herself to another husband, and Antipatros likewise to another wife, both being free from liability. And apart from these agreed terms being binding, the party who violates them shall also be subject to damages and the specified penalty.

Year 17 of Caesar, Pharmouthi 2.
